parsed_target.game_explanation: This game likely revolves around BLUE, because buffalo-green-2230 values BLUE highly at $0.70, bear-green-5745 values BLUE even more at $1.00, and goose-green-3433 values BLUE very little at $0.10. GREEN is common-value at $0.50 for everyone, so it is a natural medium of exchange. RED creates a weaker asymmetry: bear-green-5745 values RED at $0.60 while buffalo-green-2230 and goose-green-3433 each value RED at only $0.10. From an analyst view, the main gains from trade are therefore goose sending BLUE away for GREEN, and buffalo or goose sending RED to bear for GREEN or BLUE. The strategic complication is that players do not know one another's values, only their own calculator on each contemplated trade. That means proposals are shaped by trial-and-error and by trying to make offers that are attractive to at least one unknown counterparty without giving away too much. Because any accepting responder can become the trading partner, broad-based attractive offers are somewhat risky for the proposer if different responders value the offer differently. Early rounds should feature exploratory, moderately favorable offers using GREEN as payment, with later rounds adjusting to observed acceptances. The most likely pattern is that bear tries to acquire RED and possibly BLUE, buffalo tries to acquire BLUE cheaply, and goose is willing to part with BLUE for enough GREEN while being mostly indifferent between RED and BLUE except for GREEN's steady value.

rounds.round_explanation: In the opening round, players have no trade-history information, so proposals are likely to be simple probes aimed at obvious personal gains while testing whether counterparties will accept. Buffalo's strongest motive is to gain BLUE without giving up much value, but they do not know who values what, so an initial offer using low-value RED for BLUE is plausible though likely too aggressive. Bear has strong reason to seek RED, because RED is worth much more to bear than to the others, and offering GREEN is a reasonable way to entice acceptance. Goose, valuing BLUE very little, is the most natural seller of BLUE if offered enough GREEN.

After round 0, the public history suggests two useful facts: someone accepted bear's GREEN-for-RED offer, and someone accepted goose's BLUE-for-GREEN offer. Even without private-value knowledge, these events reveal that GREEN can buy concessions and that BLUE has found a seller. Buffalo should now be more confident that offering GREEN for BLUE may work. Bear may continue harvesting RED from low-RED counterparts. Goose may keep converting BLUE into GREEN if possible, though now buffalo may be especially motivated to compete for BLUE holdings.

By the final round, behavior should be more targeted. Buffalo has likely learned that goose is a willing BLUE seller for GREEN and may continue that channel if holdings permit. Bear still benefits from converting GREEN into RED whenever possible. Goose's best available self-interested move is usually to exchange remaining BLUE for GREEN, but if BLUE holdings are lower or prior offers indicate strong demand, goose may ask for a slightly better rate. Because this is the last round, players have no reason to hold back for future bargaining, so accepted trades should be closer to each sender's immediate best-value opportunities.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This game should be driven almost entirely by heterogeneous values for BLUE, because GREEN is worth the same to everyone ($0.50) and RED is also worth the same to everyone ($1.00). That means there are no gains from trade in RED or GREEN alone, but there are gains from reallocating BLUE away from players who value it less toward the player who values it most. Otter values BLUE at $0.40, Peacock at $0.50, and Cat at $0.70, so Cat is the natural buyer of BLUE and Otter is the natural seller. Peacock sits in the middle and can sometimes trade BLUE either way depending on price. Because players only know their own values, proposals will likely be framed as favorable-to-self offers that still have some chance of being privately profitable for a counterpart. The UI surplus calculator makes loss-making acceptances unlikely, so accepted trades should usually be individually profitable for both sides. A key uncertainty is that when a proposal could benefit more than one non-sender, multiple players may accept and the recipient is then randomized, so proposers may avoid very broad offers or may target trades that only one likely counterpart would want. Since RED is universally high value and GREEN is fixed at $0.50, many plausible proposals involve exchanging RED or GREEN for BLUE: Cat can profitably offer RED for enough BLUE, while Otter can profitably give BLUE away in exchange for GREEN or RED. Across three rounds, we should expect early probing offers, some declined because they are too aggressive, and then one or more accepted BLUE-reallocation trades as players learn from public history and chip constraints.

rounds.round_explanation: In the first round, players have full inventories and little information about others beyond public chip ranges. This encourages exploratory proposals. Otter, as the lowest BLUE-value player, is likely to test whether someone will part with RED or GREEN for BLUE. Peacock has weaker incentives than Cat to seek BLUE, and Cat has the strongest reason to acquire BLUE if the price is not too high. Because first-round proposals are often optimistic, at least one early trade may be declined before a more obviously mutually beneficial exchange appears.

After Cat successfully buys BLUE from Otter, the public history reveals that at least one player was willing to exchange BLUE away for RED. That makes BLUE's trading role more salient. Otter now has fewer BLUE and more RED, so may try to convert some RED into GREEN if he over-accumulated universally valuable chips. Peacock, seeing Cat's willingness to pay for BLUE, may try to sell some BLUE at an attractive rate. Cat, having already bought some BLUE, may still continue if inventories allow and the price remains favorable.

By the final round, inventories have shifted: Cat has accumulated BLUE, Peacock has shed some BLUE in exchange for RED, and Otter has already sold BLUE once. Endgame behavior is usually more transactional because there is no future strategic signaling value left. Peacock may make one last attempt to obtain more RED or unload remaining BLUE. Otter may use his final move to seek GREEN or offload residual BLUE if he still has any. Cat, now likely holding relatively more BLUE than initially, has less need to keep buying and may instead test a final opportunistic offer, though acceptable deals are scarcer because most easy BLUE gains have already been realized.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This game should revolve around bilateral gains from red and blue reallocation, with green acting as a neutral-value transfer chip. Badger values RED and BLUE equally highly at $0.80 each, so badger mainly wants to avoid giving away either red or blue cheaply and is relatively indifferent at the margin between those two colors. Camel values RED at $0.60, BLUE at $0.40, and GREEN at $0.50, so camel prefers acquiring red, is willing to part with blue, and is moderately willing to trade green depending on price. Squid values BLUE at $0.60, GREEN at $0.50, and RED at only $0.20, so squid strongly prefers acquiring blue and is the natural seller of red. The clearest surplus opportunity is therefore camel obtaining RED from squid in exchange for BLUE, because camel values red more than blue while squid values blue much more than red. Trades involving badger are harder: badger's high value for both red and blue means they will usually demand a lot for either, and counterparties may not find those prices attractive. Because players do not know one another's valuations during play, proposals are likely to test the market with simple one-color-for-one-color exchanges that are safe for the proposer under many possible recipient types. Acceptance decisions are easier than proposal decisions because responders can directly see whether the posted trade helps them. This creates uncertainty mainly on the proposing side: players may make offers that look reasonable from their own perspective but fail because the wrong chip is being targeted or the terms are too aggressive. Over three rounds, the most likely pattern is early exploratory offers, then recognition through observed acceptances that squid is willing to give up red for blue, leading to one or more accepted camel-squid trades while badger's offers are more likely to miss.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start, everyone has 10 of each chip, so inventory constraints are loose and proposals mainly reveal appetite rather than necessity. Badger is likely to test whether anyone will part with lower-valued assets for green or at a favorable red-blue ratio, but because badger privately values both red and blue highly, acceptable offers for badger are often unattractive to others. Camel has the strongest reason to seek red and can profitably pay with blue or sometimes green. Squid has the strongest reason to unload red if compensated with blue. Since no one yet knows this for sure, round 0 should feature probing offers, with the first clearly compatible camel-squid trade having the best chance of success.

After round 0, the public history shows that red-for-blue trades can clear, and holdings now reflect camel having less blue and more red, while squid has less red and more blue. This should make market inference sharper even without direct value revelation. Badger may still struggle to find a mutually beneficial deal because the two active traders now look like better counterparts for each other than for badger. Camel may continue trying to convert blue into red if any blue remains worth spending. Squid, having already improved their portfolio, may become somewhat less aggressive but can still profit from additional red-for-blue swaps.

By the final round, players have substantial information from observed acceptances. Camel and squid have likely learned, indirectly, that they are good trading partners on red versus blue. Inventories now matter more: camel has accumulated red and spent blue, while squid has done the reverse. Final-round proposals are likely to be more opportunistic and targeted. Badger still has little reason to make a concessionary offer because keeping high-valued red and blue is safe. Camel may make one last small red-for-blue attempt if blue remains. Squid may test whether anyone will overpay for the remaining low-value red, but such asks may now be less likely to clear.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This game is likely to revolve around red-for-blue exchange, with green mostly serving as a neutral fallback chip. From an analyst view, dog-blue-6080 values RED most highly ($1.00) and BLUE least among the two private colors ($0.20), so dog most wants to acquire RED and is relatively willing to give up BLUE. butterfly-blue-3807 is the mirror image in a softer form: butterfly values BLUE highly ($0.90) and RED moderately ($0.60), so butterfly most wants BLUE and can part with RED if the terms are favorable. ram-purple-9443 values both private colors weakly, especially BLUE ($0.10), and values GREEN at $0.50 like everyone else, so ram is the most likely liquidity provider: ram will often accept trades that convert BLUE into RED or GREEN, and may also offer BLUE to obtain GREEN. The main uncertainty is informational. Players do not know each other's values, only that red and blue lie somewhere in the public range. That means individually rational trades can fail if proposers ask for too much or target the wrong counterpart, and with simultaneous acceptance a proposer cannot choose which accepter receives the trade. Early rounds are therefore likely to feature cautious probes. Over time, observed acceptances reveal partial information: if butterfly accepts giving RED for BLUE, that suggests BLUE is attractive to butterfly; if ram accepts almost any BLUE-for-something trade, others may infer ram is weak on BLUE. Because all players begin symmetric in holdings and there are only three rounds, the game likely features a mix of one or two successful exploratory trades and several declined ambitious proposals rather than fully efficient reallocation.

rounds.round_explanation: In the first round, players have no behavioral evidence yet, so proposals are likely to test the market while avoiding obviously negative deals for the proposer. Dog should be inclined to offer BLUE for RED, but may start a bit ambitiously because dog does not know who values what. Butterfly has strong reason to seek BLUE and may be willing to sell RED for it. Ram is likely to act opportunistically, especially in trades that turn BLUE into either RED or GREEN.

After round 0, one informative trade has occurred: ram accepted RED-for-BLUE from butterfly. That makes it more plausible to observers that ram is a relatively cheap source of BLUE disposal and may not value RED highly. Dog still wants RED badly and now has evidence that butterfly is eager for BLUE. Butterfly, having gained BLUE, may either pause on further red sales or continue if prices remain favorable. Ram may exploit new holdings by trying to convert RED or BLUE into GREEN, which is safely valuable.

In the final round, players have limited time to hold out, so proposals may become more aggressive in trying to lock in any positive surplus. Dog has likely improved by accumulating RED and may still try one more BLUE-for-RED offer if inventory allows. Butterfly, now knowing BLUE trades can clear, may continue to prioritize BLUE but has less reason to overpay. Ram remains the player most likely to accept trades that shed BLUE or convert low-value private chips into GREEN, but others will reject anything that is plainly loss-making on their own calculator.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This game is likely to revolve around the strong gains from reallocating RED and BLUE to the players who value them most. Lion values BLUE the most at $1.00 and values RED only moderately at $0.40, while iguana values RED the most at $0.80 and values BLUE the least at $0.40. Alligator sits in the middle on BLUE at $0.60 but values RED the least at $0.30. GREEN is worth the same to everyone at $0.50, so it serves as a convenient trading medium but creates no allocative advantage by itself. The biggest private incentives are therefore: lion wants more BLUE and is relatively willing to give up RED; iguana wants more RED and is relatively willing to give up BLUE; alligator most wants to turn RED into BLUE or GREEN. Because players do not know one another's values during play, proposals are likely to be exploratory and conservative at first, with senders trying ratios that are profitable for themselves and plausibly acceptable to someone else. Since any accepting responder may be randomly selected if multiple players accept, proposers have some incentive to make offers that at least one responder should like, but they cannot target a specific partner with certainty. Over the full game, the most likely accepted trades are those where alligator offers RED for BLUE, and lion offers RED or GREEN for BLUE only if the terms are attractive enough for a responder. Iguana can profit by offering BLUE for RED, but because lion and alligator both dislike giving up RED less than iguana likes receiving it, acceptable trades may happen at moderate ratios. Overall, the likely pattern is a few accepted surplus-creating exchanges centered on RED-for-BLUE swaps, with some declined ambitious offers when the proposer asks for too much.

rounds.round_explanation: In the first round, players have full inventories and little information about others' willingness to trade. Early proposals are likely to test the market. Alligator has the clearest incentive to shed RED and acquire BLUE, since RED is only worth $0.30 to alligator and BLUE is worth $0.60. Lion wants BLUE badly but may not yet know how cheaply it can be obtained. Iguana should be open to receiving BLUE if giving up RED is compensated enough, but because RED is very valuable to iguana, low-price requests for RED are likely to fail.

By round 1, public holdings reveal some directional preferences. After the first round, alligator is likely to have shifted away from RED and toward BLUE, while iguana has accumulated more RED and lion has lost some BLUE. This makes lion's desire to replenish BLUE even stronger. Alligator may continue trying to monetize low-value RED if any remains, but now has less need. Iguana, now with more RED, may become more willing to exchange some RED away only at favorable terms, or continue trying to convert BLUE into RED if possible.

In the final round, players have less reason to experiment and more reason to make straightforward individually profitable offers. Lion still benefits substantially from acquiring BLUE, but responders may now hold fewer units they are willing to part with. Alligator may have little RED left and may instead preserve a now-improved portfolio. Iguana may probe for one more favorable RED acquisition, but any aggressive ask is likely to be declined because responders can directly see whether the trade helps them.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This game has a clear comparative-advantage structure, but the players do not know one another's exact values during play, so bargaining should mix efficient-looking offers with caution and some failed attempts. Whale-red-86 values RED most highly at $1.00 and BLUE least at $0.40, so whale should prefer trading away BLUE for RED when possible and may also tolerate swapping BLUE for GREEN if that helps. Dolphin-orange-3122 values BLUE most highly at $0.80 and RED at only $0.60, so dolphin is the natural buyer of BLUE and a plausible seller of RED. Duck-orange-4971 values RED highly at $1.00 and BLUE at $0.60, making duck somewhat similar to whale but less extreme; duck should also prefer RED over BLUE and be willing to part with BLUE for enough RED or possibly GREEN. Since GREEN is worth the same $0.50 to everyone, it can serve as a neutral medium but does not create direct private-value gains on its own unless used to exploit uncertainty about others' values. Because responders see their own exact surplus from each posted trade, they should accept only individually profitable offers. That means accepted trades are likely to be simple exchanges where the proposer gives a chip they value relatively less and asks for a chip they value relatively more, while still making the recipient better off under that recipient's private values. The main uncertainty is that proposers must guess what others might accept without knowing their exact payoffs, and when a proposal would be attractive to both opponents there is random matching risk, so players may shade offers to balance profitability against acceptance probability.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start everyone holds 10 of each chip, so inventory constraints do not yet matter much. Early proposals are likely to test the market and reveal willingness to trade. Whale should naturally try to acquire RED by giving up BLUE, because that is strongly profitable for whale and plausibly acceptable to someone who values BLUE relatively more. Dolphin then has a strong incentive to obtain BLUE and can profitably offer RED for it. Duck, seeing some history, may try to improve their RED position further, but because both opponents also like RED at least somewhat, duck may need to offer enough BLUE to get acceptance.

After round 0, the public history suggests that BLUE-for-RED exchange is feasible and that dolphin is the most likely counterparty for trades involving BLUE. Whale now has even less BLUE and more RED than initially, so whale may continue to convert BLUE into RED but could face tighter acceptance if asking terms are too aggressive. Dolphin, having shown willingness to trade RED for BLUE, may continue exploiting that pattern. Duck has learned that dolphin responds positively to sufficiently generous BLUE-for-RED offers, but duck also sees whale as a competitor for RED, which could motivate a somewhat more assertive proposal.

By the final round, players can condition on the observed pattern that only sufficiently favorable BLUE-for-RED proposals have cleared. Endgame incentives favor making acceptably generous offers rather than holding out, since there is no future bargaining value after this round. Whale should be especially motivated to convert any remaining BLUE into RED. Dolphin remains the natural demander of BLUE and likely supplier of RED, while duck may again try to gain RED but could accept that dolphin is the best realistic partner. Final-round proposals therefore likely become slightly more concessionary and more targeted toward the empirically responsive counterparty.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This game is likely to revolve around red-blue exchanges, with green serving mostly as a neutral bargaining chip. Turtle values BLUE most ($1.00) and also likes RED ($0.90), so turtle most wants to acquire blue and is relatively willing to part with red or green. Gorilla values RED most ($1.00) and values BLUE at $0.70, so gorilla most wants to acquire red and can sometimes profitably pay with blue or green. Zebra has the lowest values overall, with BLUE and GREEN both worth $0.50 and RED worth $0.60, so zebra is the most willing to accept trades that look only modestly favorable on their side. Because players know only their own values during play, proposals must be based on public ranges and inference rather than exact optimization against others' payoffs. The strongest analyst-side prediction is that gorilla can often buy red from zebra using blue, because zebra should accept any blue-for-red ratio above 0.6/0.5 = 1.2 blue per red, while gorilla profits from paying less than about 1.43 blue per red. Turtle may also try to buy blue by offering red, but this is risky because gorilla often prefers red to blue while zebra prefers blue to red, so acceptance depends on ratio. Multi-acceptance risk matters: if a sender makes an attractive public offer, more than one player could accept and the recipient is then random, so proposers may shade offers to target only the most likely counterparty. Across three rounds, likely behavior is early testing of acceptable exchange rates, then more directed proposals after the public history reveals who tends to accept which kinds of trades.

rounds.round_explanation: In the first round, players have full inventories and little information about others' willingness to trade. That usually encourages cautious but surplus-positive offers. Turtle is likely to test whether someone will part with blue in exchange for red, since turtle values blue slightly more than red. Gorilla is likely to pursue red, ideally using blue, because that is gorilla's clearest private-value arbitrage. Zebra, with relatively flat and low values, is likely to make a simple trade that improves holdings at a visible ratio rather than a sophisticated targeted offer.

After round 0, the public history suggests zebra is willing to sell red for blue at a favorable ratio, and gorilla successfully did so. Current holdings now make turtle more blue-hungry relative to gorilla and zebra more red-light. Turtle may adjust by offering green for blue, hoping zebra's low blue value makes acceptance possible. Gorilla, now holding extra red, may try to rebalance by turning some red into blue or green only if profitable on their own side. Zebra, having extra blue, may experiment with converting blue into red or green depending on visible opportunities.

By the final round, players have some revealed behavior to condition on. Gorilla has shown a consistent appetite for red and willingness to transact in blue; zebra has repeatedly accepted trades that clearly improve value at modest rates; turtle has pursued blue and may now seek one last favorable swap. Endgame behavior often becomes slightly more aggressive because there is no continuation value from preserving bargaining reputation, but each player still accepts only positive private surplus. The final sequence is likely to feature one more turtle attempt to buy blue, a gorilla effort to convert blue into red, and a zebra proposal that leverages whichever inventory has accumulated most.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This game should revolve around red-for-blue exchange opportunities, with green acting mostly as a neutral medium because it is worth $0.50 to everyone. From the analyst view, cat-green-4185 values RED highly ($0.90) and BLUE moderately ($0.60), so cat wants to acquire red and is willing to give up some blue or green, but does not want to sell red cheaply. Iguana-yellow-3262 also values RED relatively highly ($0.80) but values BLUE poorly ($0.30), so iguana is the player most willing to give away blue and most eager to pick up red. Zebra-pink-2457 values BLUE the most ($1.00) and RED the least ($0.40), so zebra is the natural red seller and blue buyer. That creates a strong bilateral complementarity between iguana and zebra, and a weaker but still possible one between cat and zebra. The main uncertainty is informational: players only know their own values, so they must infer from acceptance behavior who likes which colors. In early turns, proposers may test aggressive offers that are profitable for themselves but unattractive to others. Once a favorable red-for-blue direction is revealed through acceptance, later proposals are likely to become more targeted. Because acceptors are chosen randomly if multiple players accept, proposers also have some incentive to post offers that only one likely counterpart finds attractive. Over three rounds, the most plausible pattern is repeated zebra sales of red in exchange for blue, especially to iguana, with cat occasionally probing for red as well.

rounds.round_explanation: In the first round, players are still learning who values what. Cat likely starts by testing for red, since red is cat's highest-value chip, but may not yet know that zebra is the low-red holder. Iguana also has reason to seek red, possibly using blue as payment because blue is iguana's least valuable chip. Zebra, valuing blue highly and red poorly, is the most likely player to make a trade that others accept if zebra offers red for blue.

By round 1, one key pattern is likely visible publicly: zebra is willing to part with red for blue, and iguana is willing to give blue to get red. Cat can now make a more informed attempt to buy red, though zebra may demand a stronger blue price than cat wants to pay. Iguana may continue pursuing red because the gains remain clear from iguana's private perspective. Zebra can exploit revealed demand by posting another red-for-blue trade likely to be accepted.

In the final round, there is no future strategic benefit from posturing, so proposals should be more directly surplus-seeking. Cat still values red most and may make one last attempt if holdings permit. Iguana continues to have the strongest incentive to spend blue to obtain red. Zebra remains the natural counterparty for red sales, although zebra may become somewhat more selective after accumulating blue. The endgame likely features at least one more efficient red-for-blue exchange and one rejected aggressive offer.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This game is likely to revolve around red-blue swaps, because green is worth the same $0.50 to everyone and therefore does not create private-information gains except as a neutral bargaining chip. Bear values RED and BLUE equally at $0.80, so bear is relatively flexible and can profit by giving whichever of those chips a counterpart seems more willing to take in exchange for GREEN or by exploiting others' asymmetric values. Peacock values RED more than BLUE ($0.80 vs $0.70), so peacock prefers acquiring RED and is comparatively more willing to part with BLUE. Swan values BLUE more than RED ($0.80 vs $0.50), so swan prefers acquiring BLUE and is comparatively willing to part with RED. That means the strongest underlying gains from trade are between peacock and swan: peacock wants RED, swan wants BLUE, and each is relatively happy to give up the other item. However, because players do not know one another's exact values, proposals are made under uncertainty, and because both non-senders can accept any posted trade, proposers face competition risk when making attractive offers. Early turns are likely to test the market with mildly favorable trades for the proposer; if those fail, later turns should become more generous or more targeted based on observed acceptances. Bear's equal valuation of red and blue makes bear a likely intermediary but not a strong directional trader. Accepted trades should mostly be those where the recipient receives a chip they value more highly than the chip they surrender, with swan especially willing to sell RED for BLUE and peacock especially willing to sell BLUE for RED.

rounds.round_explanation: In the opening round, players have no direct evidence about others' willingness to trade, so proposals are likely to be exploratory. Bear moves first and may try a relatively safe offer involving GREEN, since bear values RED and BLUE highly and equally. Peacock and swan then update from what gets accepted or rejected. Because peacock privately prefers RED to BLUE and swan privately prefers BLUE to RED, the first clearly attractive cross-player deal is likely to emerge once one of them offers BLUE-for-RED or RED-for-BLUE at near-even terms.

After round 0, one informative trade has occurred: swan successfully traded away RED to get BLUE, revealing that a red-for-blue exchange can clear. Holdings now make peacock somewhat richer in RED and poorer in BLUE, and swan richer in BLUE and poorer in RED. Bear has seen this pattern and may now attempt to profit by offering GREEN for RED or BLUE, but bear still lacks strong reason to give up high-value red or blue cheaply. Peacock may continue trying to rebuild BLUE if needed, while swan may become less aggressive after already improving their portfolio.

In the final round, players have little reason to delay. Proposals should therefore become slightly more acceptance-oriented, especially if they generate clear private surplus for a likely counterpart. Bear remains the most balanced player and may try one last moderate-value swap. Peacock and swan, having already identified each other as potentially compatible traders, may again post directional red-blue offers. Still, because only one proposal is available per turn and counterparties may reject anything that is not individually profitable on their calculators, some final offers will fail if they ask for too much surplus.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This game likely revolves around blue chips, because blue has the widest disagreement in private value: gorilla-yellow-5964 values BLUE at $0.30, camel-red-6481 at $0.80, and monkey-yellow-524 at $0.90. Green is common value at $0.50 for everyone, so it is the cleanest medium of exchange and the most predictable benchmark. Red is low for monkey-yellow-524 ($0.20), modest for gorilla-yellow-5964 ($0.30), and equal to green for camel-red-6481 ($0.50). From an analyst perspective, the strongest gains from trade come when low-blue-value gorilla gives up BLUE in exchange for GREEN or sometimes RED, and when monkey gives up low-value RED for GREEN or BLUE if possible. Camel and monkey both like BLUE, so they are natural acceptors of offers that deliver BLUE, while gorilla is the natural acceptor of offers that deliver GREEN at the cost of BLUE or RED. The main uncertainty is informational: players do not know others' exact values, so they must infer willingness to trade from acceptances and rejections. That creates a likely pattern of early probing, followed by more targeted offers once players see who is willing to accept what. Because proposals are broadcast and either non-sender can accept, proposers also face competition risk: if an offer is attractive to both opponents, the realized partner is random. Over three rounds, the most plausible trajectory is several green-for-blue style trades involving gorilla, plus at least one attempt by monkey to turn low-value RED into something better, with some proposals declined when they ask too much relative to what recipients can justify on their own calculators.

rounds.round_explanation: In the opening round, players have no behavioral evidence yet, so offers are likely to be exploratory but still grounded in each sender's own calculator. Gorilla-yellow-5964 has the clearest motive to shed BLUE because it is worth only $0.30 to them, while GREEN is worth $0.50. Camel-red-6481 and monkey-yellow-524 both value BLUE highly, so an offer that gives them BLUE for GREEN can attract acceptance. Camel may also test whether others will part with RED or BLUE for GREEN, since camel values GREEN and RED equally and BLUE highly. Monkey's strongest private motive is to get rid of low-value RED and try to obtain GREEN or BLUE.

By round 1, players have some revealed-preference evidence. Gorilla has shown willingness to trade away BLUE for GREEN, and monkey has shown willingness to offer extra RED to obtain GREEN. This should sharpen expectations. Gorilla is still likely to seek more GREEN by selling BLUE, since that remains profitable. Camel has learned that others may not surrender RED cheaply for GREEN alone, so a camel proposal may target BLUE less aggressively or offer a deal favorable enough to induce acceptance. Monkey, after successfully moving some RED, may try either another RED-for-GREEN conversion or a GREEN-for-BLUE upgrade if holdings allow, but acceptance depends heavily on the counterpart's private values.

In the final round, strategic delay matters less because there is no future bargaining value to preserve. Players should make more direct surplus-seeking offers. Camel moves first this round and may exploit the public evidence that gorilla repeatedly parts with BLUE. Gorilla then still has incentive to convert remaining BLUE into GREEN if possible. Monkey, moving last, can condition on current holdings and the revealed market: if earlier offers showed that GREEN is the easiest way to buy cooperation, monkey may again try to move RED for GREEN, though final-round proposals can still fail if they are too one-sided for recipients' calculators.

rounds.round_explanation: In the first round, players have no behavioral history yet, so proposals are likely to test the market. Duck is the weakest holder of BLUE in value terms and may try to use BLUE to buy a chip with more certain value such as GREEN. Lion has moderate values and may make a balanced offer that is only mildly favorable to the recipient. Tiger, who values BLUE and RED highly, is the most likely to post a clearly acceptable trade using GREEN as payment because GREEN is equally valued by everyone.

After seeing tiger successfully buy BLUE with GREEN, players can infer that GREEN-for-BLUE trades may clear, especially with duck. Duck now has extra GREEN and less BLUE, so may try to convert GREEN into RED, which duck values more than GREEN. Lion may continue probing for advantageous swaps but is constrained by the fact that counterparties only accept positive own surplus. Tiger, having already added BLUE, may now target RED from lion or duck using GREEN, since RED is also especially valuable to tiger.

By the final round, players have observed that tiger's offers can be acceptable when they compensate with enough GREEN, while stingier proposals tend to fail. Duck has already sold some BLUE and may now be reluctant to give up RED cheaply. Lion has just sold RED and may seek BLUE or RED back only on very favorable terms. parsed_target.game_explanation: This game has a strong but incomplete gains-from-trade structure. Bear-yellow-1294 and goose-green-2832 both value RED highly ($1.00) and BLUE relatively little ($0.30 and $0.20), while ram-blue-9899 has the reverse pattern on BLUE ($1.00) and somewhat lower value on RED ($0.80). GREEN is common value at $0.50 for everyone, so green mainly serves as a neutral medium rather than a source of asymmetric surplus. From an analyst perspective, the biggest efficient direction is for BLUE to move from bear or goose to ram, and for RED to move from ram to bear or goose. However, players do not know each other's values, so they must infer willingness from observed acceptances and rejections. Because acceptance is based on each responder's private calculator, proposals must give the responder nonnegative surplus, and usually some positive surplus to be reliably accepted. Early in the game, bear and goose are likely to test whether others will part with RED for GREEN or low amounts of BLUE, but ram has weak incentives to sell RED cheaply because RED is still worth $0.80 to ram. Ram is likely to discover that asking for BLUE in exchange for RED is attractive to at least one of the others, especially bear, since bear gives up low-value BLUE for high-value RED. Across three rounds, the likely dynamic is initial probing and some failed proposals, followed by trades where ram supplies RED in exchange for BLUE, with bear the most willing counterparty. Goose also likes acquiring RED, but goose's BLUE is even less valuable privately, so goose may try aggressive offers that are too stingy for others to accept. Holdings updates matter because once bear transfers BLUE to ram and accumulates RED, ram becomes even less likely to accept green-based offers and more likely to keep exploiting its informationally discovered leverage over RED-for-BLUE exchanges.

rounds.round_explanation: In the opening round, players are uncertain about others' private values, so proposals are likely to be exploratory. Bear and goose both know they personally love RED and care relatively little about BLUE, but they do not know who values RED less. Ram knows BLUE is extremely valuable and may be reluctant to give it up or accept offers involving BLUE outflows. Because GREEN has equal value for all players, green-for-red offers are tempting for red-hungry players to attempt, but such offers are unlikely to satisfy a responder unless the quantities are generous.

After round 0, everyone has seen that ram could successfully trade away RED for BLUE, revealing at least one red-hungry counterpart. Bear now holds more RED and less BLUE, while ram holds more BLUE and less RED. This history should make bear and goose more inclined to seek RED again, but also make ram more aware that RED can be sold at favorable terms. Proposals are therefore likely to center on RED-for-BLUE bargaining, with green continuing to look weak as payment.

By the final round, the trade pattern is clearer: ram can profitably exchange RED for BLUE, while bear and goose both benefit from obtaining RED if the price is not too high. Because there are no future rounds after this one, players may make slightly more decisive offers instead of pure experimentation. Bear may continue to chase RED despite having already acquired some, goose may try one more cheap attempt, and ram is likely to post another RED-for-BLUE offer calibrated to be acceptable to at least one counterpart.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This game likely revolves around red-blue exchange, with green mostly acting as a neutral numeraire rather than a source of true gains. Uncertainty comes from each player not knowing others' exact values, from competition between the two non-senders when an offer is broadly acceptable, and from early rejected offers serving as crude signals. rounds.round_explanation: At the start, all three players hold 10 of each chip, but nobody knows others' private values. Round 0 is therefore likely to feature exploratory proposals. Badger may try to buy BLUE because it is especially valuable to him, but a demanding offer may fail if others also happen to like BLUE. Peacock has the strongest reason to seek RED and can cheaply part with BLUE, making a red-for-blue offer especially plausible. Unicorn likewise benefits from converting RED into BLUE, but may first test whether others will overpay for RED or accept a modest red-for-blue exchange.

After round 0, some information has been revealed through behavior. Peacock successfully traded away BLUE to get RED, which is consistent with low BLUE value and strong RED demand. Unicorn then offered RED for BLUE, signaling that BLUE is attractive to him and RED is relatively expendable. Badger accepted that trade, showing willingness to part with RED for BLUE. With these public actions and updated holdings, round 1 should feature more targeted red-blue bargaining, with green mostly secondary unless used to sweeten an offer.

By the final round, players have substantial behavioral evidence. Peacock has repeatedly shown appetite for RED and willingness to give BLUE. Badger has repeatedly pursued BLUE. Unicorn has tried to turn RED into BLUE but may find counterparties less willing at his preferred prices. Final-round incentives can cut both ways: some players may become more concessionary to avoid ending with low-value chips, while others may hold out if they already improved their position. A plausible finish is one last successful red-for-blue trade involving Peacock, with other ambitious asks being rejected.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This game is likely to revolve around BLUE chips. From an analyst perspective, owl-purple-3001 values BLUE the most at $0.70, mouse-green-4201 values BLUE at $0.40, and dog-orange-6603 values BLUE the least at $0.30. GREEN is worth the same to everyone at $0.50, and RED is worth $0.50 to dog-orange-6603 and owl-purple-3001 but only $0.40 to mouse-green-4201. That creates a predictable surplus pattern: owl-purple-3001 should want to acquire BLUE aggressively, especially by paying with RED or GREEN; dog-orange-6603 is the natural seller of BLUE; and mouse-green-4201 has weaker incentives because their values are more balanced. However, players do not know others' exact values during play, so proposals must be based on beliefs about what others might accept. Since recipients only accept trades that are privately profitable, proposers are likely to test modestly favorable offers first. Multi-acceptance risk also matters: if a proposal is attractive to both opponents, the proposer cannot choose which accepter gets picked. Because all players begin with equal chip endowments and there are only 3 rounds with 3 proposal turns each, bargaining should feature simple bilateral-looking exchanges, especially BLUE-for-RED or BLUE-for-GREEN. The most likely successful trades are those where owl-purple-3001 buys BLUE and where mouse-green-4201 tries to convert lower-valued RED into GREEN or BLUE at favorable rates. Dog-orange-6603 may try to acquire GREEN or RED by giving BLUE only if the terms are generous enough for the recipient, but dog's own incentives make them reluctant to give away too much value.

rounds.round_explanation: In the first round, players are still learning from market feedback, so proposals are likely to probe willingness to trade rather than fully exploit known surplus. Dog-orange-6603, as the lowest BLUE valuer, has the clearest case for offering BLUE in exchange for another chip, but may not know how generous the offer must be. Mouse-green-4201 has mild incentives to move away from RED, which they value least. Owl-purple-3001 is the strongest BLUE buyer and is the most likely player to make a trade that others are willing to accept.

After seeing owl-purple-3001 successfully buy BLUE with RED, the strategic pattern becomes clearer. Dog-orange-6603 now has evidence that BLUE can be sold, but because dog has already reduced BLUE holdings, they may ask for stronger terms. Mouse-green-4201 may infer that BLUE is in demand and either try to sell BLUE or continue trying to unload RED. Owl-purple-3001, encouraged by prior success, remains the most likely to post an acceptable offer for BLUE, possibly using GREEN this time because GREEN is common value and easier for others to evaluate.

By the final round, the history strongly suggests that owl-purple-3001 is the active BLUE demander and dog-orange-6603 is the main BLUE supplier. Dog-orange-6603 now has fewer BLUE chips and may be less willing to part with them except at favorable terms. Mouse-green-4201, having failed to trade earlier, may make one last attempt at a modest profitable swap. Owl-purple-3001 may still chase BLUE, but diminishing inventories and stricter final-round bargaining could lead to more rejected offers.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This game is likely to be relatively cautious and only moderately active because the value structure is nearly aligned across players. Alligator-orange-6315 and buffalo-purple-4069 have identical values: RED is best at $1.00, BLUE is second at $0.70, and GREEN is worst at $0.50. Rabbit-yellow-6681 has the same ranking but slightly lower values for RED ($0.90) and BLUE ($0.60), with GREEN still $0.50. Because everyone ranks chips in the same order, there are no strong gains from specialization. Most mutually beneficial trades will therefore come from small differences in intensity, especially with rabbit being a slightly cheaper source of RED or BLUE relative to the other two. Green is common-value and weak for everyone, so players may try to pay with GREEN to obtain RED or BLUE, but such offers will usually be unattractive unless the quantities are favorable. Since responders see their own net surplus exactly, negative offers should be rejected consistently. Another important feature is competition between responders: if a posted offer is profitable for both non-senders, both may accept, and a random recipient is chosen. That creates uncertainty for proposers, who may craft offers attractive to either counterpart. Still, because proposer and responder incentives are fairly transparent once a trade appears, the likely path is a mix of exploratory opening offers, selective acceptance of clearly positive trades, and later adjustment as holdings change. Alligator and buffalo should often seek RED-preserving or RED-acquiring deals, while rabbit is somewhat more willing to part with RED when compensated in larger quantities of BLUE or GREEN, though only if the arithmetic is favorable.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start, all players hold 10 of each chip, so no one is constrained by inventory. With no history yet, early proposals are likely to test whether others will accept favorable GREEN-for-BLUE or GREEN-for-RED exchanges. Because alligator and buffalo share identical values, neither is likely to accept deals that give away RED cheaply. Rabbit has slightly lower values on RED and BLUE, so the first accepted trade is most likely one where rabbit sells RED or BLUE at a price that is good for rabbit but even better for one of the high-value players.

After round 0, rabbit has converted one RED into two BLUE, which is profitable for rabbit, while one of the high-value players has improved by acquiring RED. The visible history now suggests that rabbit may be a plausible trading partner for RED. Alligator, if it received the earlier trade, now has even more reason to avoid selling RED and may instead try to offload GREEN for BLUE. Buffalo, having seen rabbit's willingness to part with RED, may target rabbit with a more favorable GREEN-for-RED offer, but rabbit will still reject any offer that is calculator-negative. As holdings diverge slightly, proposals may become a bit more tailored.

By the final round, players can condition strongly on the public history. Rabbit has shown some willingness to trade RED away when the compensation in BLUE is good enough, and the two high-value players have likely learned that simple GREEN-for-good-chip offers are too aggressive. Final-round proposals should therefore be either clearly profitable to at least one responder or they will be ignored. Since there is no continuation value after this round, proposers may make their best surplus-seeking offers rather than preserve reputation. Accepted trades are still most likely to involve rabbit on one side and either alligator or buffalo on the other, because that is where the small valuation wedge exists.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This game likely revolves around red chips, because valuations for green are identical at $0.50 for everyone and blue is worth only $0.50 to goose-green-3433 and bear-green-5745 but just $0.20 to buffalo-green-2230. Red is especially valuable to goose-green-3433 ($0.90) and also valuable to buffalo-green-2230 ($0.80), while bear-green-5745 is indifferent between red, blue, and green at $0.50 each. That makes bear-green-5745 the most natural liquidity provider and buffalo-green-2230 the most likely trader willing to part with blue cheaply. Since players only know their own values, proposals must be based on guesses about what others might accept. A likely dynamic is that goose-green-3433 tries to acquire red or avoid giving it up cheaply, bear-green-5745 makes relatively even trades that are attractive to others, and buffalo-green-2230 tries to exchange low-value blue for red or green. Because acceptances are based on private surplus and multiple acceptors create random matching, proposers have incentive to post trades that at least one counterpart may find profitable. Still, there is substantial uncertainty because goose-green-3433 does not know that buffalo-green-2230 values blue very little, and buffalo-green-2230 does not know exactly how much goose-green-3433 values red. The most plausible full-game path therefore has modest, individually profitable trades centered on blue-for-red or blue-for-green exchanges, with some failed proposals when a sender asks too much or offers a chip the others do not value enough.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start, everyone holds 10 of each chip, so no one faces inventory pressure. Early proposals are likely to probe counterpart values. Goose-green-3433 highly values red and should be reluctant to sell it, but may try to buy red using blue or green if they believe someone is indifferent. Bear-green-5745, who values all chips equally, can profitably accept any trade that gives more chip-value than it costs, regardless of color. Buffalo-green-2230 values blue least and is the most likely accepter of offers that trade away red or green in exchange for enough blue or to reject requests for red unless compensated well.

After round 0, bear-green-5745 has become a red seller and accumulated extra blue, while goose-green-3433 has increased red holdings and buffalo-green-2230 has also improved by converting low-value blue into red. Public history now suggests that bear-green-5745 may be willing to part with red near even rates, though the others still do not know bear-green-5745's exact indifference. Goose-green-3433 is likely to continue trying to buy red, while buffalo-green-2230 may shift to acquiring green if red seems harder to obtain. Some proposals may fail if they are only attractive to one specific private type and that player does not hold enough inventory or sees too little gain.

By the final round, players can condition on revealed willingness to trade. Goose-green-3433 has reason to keep converting medium-value chips into red whenever possible. Bear-green-5745 remains the most flexible trader and may accept any favorable quantity imbalance. Buffalo-green-2230 has likely already exploited blue's low private value, but may still search for another red-for-blue deal if inventory permits. Because this is the last round, there is less reason to experiment and more reason to post straightforward surplus-positive trades aimed at the most likely accepting counterpart.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This game should revolve around red chips. Using the analyst-side values, peacock-red-3668 values RED most at $0.80 and BLUE at $0.50, so peacock prefers acquiring red and dislikes giving it up cheaply. Otter-blue-7516 also values RED highly at $0.70, while cat-orange-2462 values RED only at $0.50, the same as GREEN and more than BLUE at $0.30. GREEN is common value at $0.50 for everyone, so green is the natural 'currency' chip: it creates no hidden-value advantage by itself, but it can be used to make acceptable trades. BLUE is privately heterogeneous, but in this profile nobody values BLUE above GREEN, so blue is not especially attractive to request unless someone suspects others value it less. The main efficient direction is that cat is the player most willing to part with RED, while peacock and otter are the likely buyers of RED. However, proposers face uncertainty because they do not know others' values and any accepted offer can be taken by either non-sender. That creates incentives to post offers that are individually profitable and broadly acceptable, but not so generous that they overpay. As the game progresses and accepted trades reveal some willingness to exchange RED for GREEN, later proposers can update beliefs from public history and current holdings. Because there are only three rounds and one proposal per player per round, the likely pattern is cautious opening offers, then somewhat more targeted red-for-green proposals once players infer who may be willing to trade.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start all players hold 10 of each color, so nobody is inventory-constrained. With no revealed history, first-round proposals are likely to test the market. Since GREEN has common value and RED is plausibly desirable to many own-value types, early offers may ask for RED while paying in GREEN. Cat is the most plausible seller of RED given the analyst-side values, but peacock and otter do not know that for sure, so opening proposals may be slightly conservative to avoid overpaying.

After round 0, public history suggests that red-for-green exchanges are feasible and that cat is willing to release RED at close to parity with GREEN. Peacock now has evidence that simple green-for-red offers can work, while otter has also successfully bought RED. Because accepted offers can be sniped by either non-sender, proposers still face competition risk and may keep offers modest but acceptable. Holdings begin to matter slightly: cat has accumulated more GREEN and fewer RED, while peacock and otter have both increased RED.

In the final round, strategic delay is less useful because there are no future opportunities after these turns. That often makes acceptable offers slightly more likely to be accepted if they generate positive surplus. By now the public record strongly indicates that cat is the marginal seller of RED and that peacock and otter both want more RED. Since cat's RED holdings have fallen over time, cat may become less able or less willing to continue selling large amounts, so final-round trades are likely to be small. Proposers should still avoid offering BLUE for RED because GREEN is the safer payment chip under private-value uncertainty.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This game should be driven mainly by asymmetries in red and blue values. Squid values BLUE very highly and RED very little, so squid is the clearest natural seller of red and buyer of blue. Badger values both RED and BLUE at $1.00, making badger flexible and willing to acquire either high-value color if the offered terms are favorable. Camel values BLUE at $0.80 and RED at $0.40, so camel is more interested in blue than red but less intensely than squid or badger. GREEN is worth the same $0.50 to everyone, so it works as a neutral medium of exchange but does not create private-information advantages. Because responses are simultaneous and the proposer cannot choose among multiple acceptors, proposals that are profitable for more than one opponent carry matching uncertainty. With these values, likely efficient-looking directions are squid offering RED to get BLUE or GREEN, badger trying to turn GREEN into BLUE or RED, and camel trying to obtain BLUE using RED or GREEN. However, each player only knows their own values, so they must infer others' willingness from observed acceptances and rejections. Early turns are therefore likely to feature somewhat cautious or exploratory offers, followed by more targeted proposals once the public history reveals what kinds of trades clear. Since all players begin with large equal endowments of 10 of each chip, liquidity constraints are mild, and the game is likely to include a mix of one-for-one and slightly tilted trades that preserve positive surplus for the proposer while being acceptable to at least one responder.

rounds.round_explanation: In the opening round, players have no behavioral evidence yet, so proposals are likely to test broad acceptability. Squid has the strongest reason to shed RED and seek BLUE or GREEN. Badger can profit from many exchanges but may start with a moderate trade using GREEN as payment because it is lower value than RED or BLUE to badger. Camel, moving last, can condition on the first two outcomes and may try to turn relatively lower-value RED into BLUE if enough inventory remains among the others.

By round 1, the history suggests that badger is willing to acquire red for blue at favorable rates, and camel is willing to part with blue for enough green. Squid should now be more confident about targeting red-for-blue trades. Badger may continue converting lower-value GREEN into higher-value chips. Camel learned that asking directly for BLUE with RED alone was too aggressive, so a more balanced RED-for-GREEN proposal is more plausible.

In the final round, there is no future strategic value to holding out, so proposals can become more surplus-focused and slightly more aggressive if they still leave at least one responder willing to accept. Squid still most wants BLUE and is happy to spend RED. Badger remains the most versatile trader and can exploit either opponent's willingness to exchange against GREEN. Camel may make one last attempt to upgrade RED into GREEN, which should be attractive to squid because squid strongly dislikes RED.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This game is likely to revolve around large differences in red and blue values. Ram values RED highly ($0.90) and BLUE moderately ($0.70), so ram has little reason to give up red cheaply and prefers acquiring blue or green only when the exchange is favorable. Dog values BLUE very little ($0.10) and RED also low ($0.20), with GREEN at $0.50, so dog is the natural seller of blue and reluctant seller of green; dog can profit by exchanging blue for almost anything else, but may also make overreaching proposals because dog does not know others' values. Butterfly is intermediate, valuing RED at $0.70 and BLUE/GREEN at $0.50, so butterfly is the most flexible trader and is often the likely accepting party when offered enough red or green for blue. Because each responder sees exact private surplus from a posted trade, individually loss-making offers should be declined reliably. The main uncertainty is that proposers know only their own values, so they may test aggressive terms and learn from rejections. Given the profiles, mutually beneficial trades are especially likely when dog offers BLUE for GREEN or RED, and somewhat likely when butterfly offers BLUE to ram in exchange for RED or when ram offers GREEN for BLUE. Trades demanding RED from ram are hard because ram values RED the most. Overall, the forecast is a game with some early probing, then selective acceptance of proposals that exploit dog's very low blue value and butterfly's balanced portfolio preferences.

rounds.round_explanation: In the first round, players are likely to probe the market with imperfect beliefs about others' values. Ram may try to turn lower-valued green into blue, which ram likes more. Dog is incentivized to offer blue away because it is nearly worthless to dog, and such offers have a good chance of attracting an accept. Butterfly may make a moderate, flexible proposal, but with no prior history may still test somewhat ambitious terms.

By round 1, the accepted dog-butterfly trade reveals that blue can move for green, so players may shade proposals toward that exchange rate. Ram still has little incentive to surrender red, but may continue targeting blue using green. Dog, now with extra green and less blue, still values blue very little and may try another blue-for-green trade. Butterfly may use accumulated blue to seek red again, but likely at terms that remain too weak for ram.

In the final round, players have some evidence that dog is the easiest counterparty for blue trades and that ram will not part with red on modest terms. Ram may make one last efficient green-for-blue offer. Dog may continue monetizing blue inventory, possibly toward ram this time if holdings align. Butterfly, seeing red hard to obtain, may pivot to a safer proposal involving blue for green, though final-round ambition could still produce rejection if terms are too demanding.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This game is likely to revolve around BLUE chips. Lion values RED and BLUE equally low at $0.20 each, while Iguana and Alligator both value BLUE highly at $0.70. GREEN is common value at $0.50 for everyone, so GREEN can serve as a relatively safe trading currency but does not create much private-value advantage by itself. Lion is the player with the strongest incentive to convert BLUE into other chips, since BLUE is worth only $0.20 to Lion but $0.70 to the other two. Iguana and Alligator may both try to obtain BLUE from Lion, creating competition on Lion's turns and some uncertainty for proposers because if both accept, the recipient is randomly chosen. RED creates weaker gains: Iguana values RED at $0.50, Alligator at $0.30, and Lion at $0.20, so Lion can sometimes profitably sell RED as well, especially to Iguana. Because players only know their own values during play, Iguana and Alligator may infer from accepted or rejected offers who is likely to value which colors more, but they still face uncertainty about exact counterpart incentives. A likely overall pattern is that Lion proposes favorable exchanges that unload BLUE or RED for GREEN, Iguana tries to turn GREEN into RED or BLUE when profitable, and Alligator mainly seeks BLUE while being less willing than Iguana to pay much for RED. Most accepted trades should be individually profitable on both sides according to each responder's calculator, with some modestly ambitious offers declined when the requested quantity is too high.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start, all players have balanced endowments, so early offers are likely to test the market. Lion has the clearest arbitrage opportunity because Lion can give away low-value BLUE or RED in exchange for GREEN. Iguana and Alligator both like BLUE, so Lion can ask for GREEN and expect at least one acceptance if the ratio is attractive. Iguana then has an incentive to use GREEN to buy RED or BLUE, since both are worth at least as much or more to Iguana than GREEN. Alligator's best opportunities are mostly to buy BLUE, but with Lion moving first, some of that opportunity may already be reduced.

After Lion's successful first trade, Lion now has fewer BLUE and more GREEN, while Iguana has demonstrated willingness to accept BLUE-for-GREEN exchanges. Failed offers from Iguana and Alligator suggest that asking too much for RED or BLUE will be rejected. In this round, Lion is likely to continue making efficient low-risk offers, perhaps unloading RED this time. Iguana may adjust by making a more responder-friendly offer for RED, especially targeting Lion, who values RED least. Alligator remains motivated to acquire BLUE but may need to improve the terms compared with the previous declined attempt.

By the final round, trade history has revealed some useful patterns: Lion has repeatedly accepted deals that exchange low-value RED or BLUE for higher-value GREEN, Iguana has shown strong interest in both BLUE and RED, and Alligator has shown strongest interest in BLUE. Final-round behavior is likely to be slightly more aggressive because there is no future opportunity cost. Lion may again try to monetize remaining BLUE. Iguana may seek one more profitable acquisition if holdings permit. Alligator may continue to chase BLUE, but if Lion has already sold much of it, Alligator may either offer stronger terms or face another rejection.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This game has a clear but incomplete source of gains from trade. Green is worth $0.50 to everyone, so it serves as a neutral benchmark chip. Red creates the main asymmetry: whale-red-86 values RED highly at $0.70, dolphin-orange-3122 values RED at $0.50, and duck-orange-4971 values RED only at $0.20. Blue is relatively low for everyone, but whale-red-86 values BLUE slightly more ($0.30) than the other two ($0.20). As an analyst, the strongest prior is that whale-red-86 will try to acquire RED from duck-orange-4971, because duck is the lowest-red holder and whale is the highest-red valuer. Trades involving GREEN can also work as a numeraire-like exchange medium because its value is common and known only through each player's own calculator outcome. Dolphin-orange-3122 is comparatively flexible: RED and GREEN are equally valuable to dolphin, while BLUE is less valuable, so dolphin is likely willing to trade away BLUE and may also be approximately indifferent between holding RED and GREEN. Duck-orange-4971 should be willing to sell RED at modest prices because RED is low value to duck, but duck will not knowingly accept negative-surplus offers. Uncertainty comes from private values: players do not know exactly why others accept or reject, so proposals may begin somewhat exploratory and conservative. Because multiple acceptors are randomized, proposers also have some incentive to post offers that more than one opponent might accept, though in this profile most profitable offers are likely targeted implicitly at one specific counterpart. Over three rounds, the likely pattern is early probing, then one or two successful whale acquisitions of RED from duck or dolphin, with some failed offers when proposers ask for too much surplus.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start all players hold 10 RED, 10 BLUE, and 10 GREEN. The first round is likely to feature price discovery. Dolphin-orange-3122 has little reason to keep BLUE relative to RED or GREEN, so an opening offer that gives BLUE for RED is plausible. Whale-red-86 has the strongest incentive to obtain RED and may test whether someone will part with RED for GREEN or BLUE. Duck-orange-4971 is the natural seller of RED, but duck does not know others' values, so duck may also start with a simple, safe trade that converts low-value RED into GREEN.

After round 0, whale-red-86 has already accumulated extra RED, duck-orange-4971 has converted some RED into GREEN, and dolphin-orange-3122 has learned that other players were not eager to sell RED cheaply for BLUE. The second round likely reflects this new public history. Dolphin may continue trying to upgrade BLUE into GREEN or RED. Whale is likely to press its informational advantage from observed acceptances and make another red-acquisition attempt. Duck, seeing that whale has accepted red-buying trades, may try another sale of RED at a favorable rate.

By the final round, the broad pattern should be apparent from public holdings: whale-red-86 has been accumulating RED and duck-orange-4971 has been shedding RED. That makes further whale-red trades still plausible, but duck's remaining RED stock is lower and bargaining positions are firmer. Dolphin-orange-3122 may try one last efficient conversion of BLUE into GREEN, though that requires someone else to value the exchange positively. Whale may still find one more modest RED purchase worthwhile. Duck's final proposal is likely to be ambitious or thinly profitable and may fail if the remaining counterpart is no longer interested at the posted terms.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This game has a strong comparative-advantage structure. Zebra values RED most highly ($0.80) and BLUE least ($0.30). Gorilla values GREEN and BLUE more than RED, with RED especially low for them ($0.20). Turtle values BLUE most highly ($0.70) and RED least ($0.10). Because GREEN is common-value at $0.50 for everyone, it often serves as a middle-value trading chip. From an analyst view, the main efficient direction is RED flowing toward zebra, BLUE flowing toward turtle, and some GREEN moving to whichever side helps bridge trades. But the players do not know each other's exact values; they only infer from acceptances and rejections. That creates uncertainty about how aggressive proposals should be. Since responders see their own exact payoff change, they should reject negative-surplus trades and usually accept positive-surplus trades. Proposers therefore have incentives to make offers that are clearly profitable for at least one unknown counterpart, but not overly generous. Early in the game, players are likely to test the market with trades that exploit their own obvious low-value holdings: zebra offering BLUE for RED, gorilla offering RED for BLUE or GREEN, and turtle offering RED or GREEN for BLUE. Across three rounds, accepted trades should gradually reveal who is willing to part with which chips, making later proposals more targeted. A likely overall pattern is zebra successfully acquiring RED from gorilla, turtle acquiring BLUE from zebra, and gorilla using RED or GREEN strategically to pick up BLUE or other favorable assets. Holdings constraints also matter: successful early trades reduce the stock of the most useful barter chips, so later proposals may become smaller or shift toward GREEN-mediated exchanges.

rounds.round_explanation: In the first round, players have no game-specific history, so proposals are likely to be exploratory but still grounded in each sender's own values. Zebra is naturally motivated to turn low-value BLUE into high-value RED, though they must guess whether anyone else likes BLUE enough. Gorilla has the clearest cheap asset in RED and may try converting it into BLUE or GREEN. Turtle most values BLUE and least values RED, so turtle may also try to obtain BLUE by giving up RED. Because responders accept based on their own payoff calculators, round 0 should feature offers that are modestly favorable to likely partners rather than extreme gambles.

After round 0, some information has been revealed. Zebra successfully traded away BLUE for RED and then accepted RED-for-BLUE from turtle, confirming zebra's willingness to keep converting BLUE into RED. Gorilla accepted BLUE in exchange for RED, suggesting BLUE is indeed useful to them, but their later attempt to sell RED for GREEN failed, indicating others will not overpay for RED. Turtle showed strong demand for BLUE. In this round, proposals are likely to become more targeted: zebra may again approach gorilla with a BLUE-for-RED trade, gorilla may try to use low-value RED to extract more BLUE, and turtle may continue offering RED to anyone holding spare BLUE.

By the final round, bargaining is constrained by updated holdings and by what players have learned from repeated acceptances and rejections. Zebra has repeatedly shown willingness to give up BLUE and take in RED, but may now also accept GREEN if priced well because BLUE remains their least-valued chip. Gorilla has learned that simply asking for BLUE with too little offered may fail, so they may sweeten terms or pivot to GREEN-based deals. Turtle still wants BLUE but may face scarcity if zebra has already reduced BLUE holdings; turtle may need to offer more attractive terms. Final-round proposals often become either more efficient and realistic or more opportunistic because there is no future retaliation or reputation to preserve.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This game has strong gains-from-trade structure driven by opposite red/blue preferences. cat-green-4185 values BLUE most ($0.70) and values RED/GREEN equally at $0.50, iguana-yellow-3262 values RED most ($0.80) and BLUE least ($0.30), and zebra-pink-2457 values BLUE most ($0.80) and RED least ($0.40). GREEN is common value at $0.50 for everyone, so it works as a neutral medium of exchange but does not create surplus by itself. The main efficient pattern is that iguana wants to give away BLUE and receive RED, while zebra wants to give away RED and receive BLUE; cat also prefers BLUE to RED and is roughly indifferent between RED and GREEN, so cat can also seek BLUE by offering RED or GREEN. Because players only know their own values, proposals must be based on beliefs about what others might accept. Since responders see their own surplus exactly, they should accept individually profitable offers and decline losses. This creates uncertainty for proposers: a trade that is attractive to the sender may still fail if the requested chip is highly valued by possible recipients. Early in the game, players are likely to test ambitious trades, especially asking for BLUE in exchange for RED or GREEN. Over time, the public history and updated holdings should push proposals toward simpler, more obviously acceptable exchanges.
